title: Tokens
description: Factory and work tokens: the unit of submitted work that occupies a work-type state as it moves through you-agent-factory.
openingSummary: A factory token, also called a work token, is the runtime unit that represents one submitted work item while it occupies a work-type state.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A factory token, also called a work token, is the runtime unit that represents one submitted work item occupying a work-type state. That state is called a place: the factory tracks where each token sits by the place it occupies. The token is the thing the runtime moves — not a chat message, and not an LLM tokenizer piece.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: When you read whether a workstation is enabled, inspect you work list or session work views, or ask why a submit lands work in an initial state, you are looking at factory tokens. Recognizing that unit of work makes those surfaces readable without reverse-engineering the rest of the topology first.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: You submit work for a task work type. The runtime places a token in the initial place task:init. An enabled workstation consumes that token, a worker runs, and the outcome routes the token to another place such as accepted, continue, rejection, or failure.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: Tokens show up wherever the factory talks about places and routing: work types and states in factory.json form places named like task:init, workstation inputs and outputs consume and emit tokens, and inspection surfaces such as you work list or session work views show which tokens sit in which states.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Factory and work tokens are not LLM tokenizer or model-input tokens. This page means the unit of work moving through the factory, not pieces of text a model reads. Design-system or UI tokens are also out of scope here.
